headlines.geopolitics: Community honors 'unclaimed' Navy veteran with full military funeral in Tennessee
headlines.economy: DOJ creates new fraud division targeting 'insane' Medicaid and federal program scams
headlines.domestic: New poll shows 87% expect AI to transform America, but most don't find it useful yet

metadata.daily_do: Check if there's an unclaimed veteran ceremony in your area this month — many happen quarterly and always need attendance

clips.caption: When the community shows up for forgotten veterans
DOJ's new approach to fraud: prosecute everything over $1000
Jax explains why his boss might be an alien

script.text: The Romans had a saying: 'No soldier should die twice.' First in battle, then in forgetting.
Good morning, it's Monday, March 10th. I'm Leo Barros, and this is The Friction. Here's what happened while you were sleeping off your weekend.

First up — geopolitics with a twist. In Tennessee, hundreds of people showed up to bury Navy veteran Lonnie Wayman, who had no known family. The VA called him 'unclaimed,' but the community said otherwise. Full military honors, packed ceremony, nobody buried alone.
Oh man, that's beautiful.
That's how it should be.

Economy — Deputy AG Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division. Target? What he calls the 'insane' fraud problem in federal programs. Two Pennsylvania guys got four million dollars for fake Minneapolis homeless shelters. New policy: steal over a thousand bucks, go to prison.
Four million for fake homeless shelters? I can barely get my security deposit back.
Wait, what about the actual homeless people in Minneapolis?

Domestic — new Fox News poll on AI. Eighty-seven percent of Americans think AI will transform our lives, but only forty-seven percent find it useful today. Eighty-nine percent want AI content clearly labeled. Oh, and seventy-four percent of Americans think life exists on other planets.
So we're not ready for robot overlords but we're cool with alien neighbors. Got it.
The alien thing makes more sense than trusting AI to write my emails.
Fermi's paradox suggests we should be more concerned about the silence than the possibility.
Doc just casually dropped the most terrifying thing anyone's said today.
Alright, let's start with the story that made me believe in humanity again. Tennessee.

So here's what happened. Lonnie Wayman, Navy veteran, dies. No known family claims his remains. He gets labeled 'unclaimed' by the VA. But word gets out, and hundreds of people show up to his funeral. Full military honors. The VA rep at the ceremony said quote, 'the word unclaimed doesn't fit what's unfolding before us.'
This is what community looks like when it actually functions. Like, this is the social safety net that isn't on any government org chart.
It's also what happens when people remember what service means. This guy served his country, and his country showed up for him.
Can I just say this is way better than those viral TikToks where people film themselves doing good deeds? Like, nobody was livestreaming this funeral for clout.
But it raises the question — should the government be doing more to track down families? Or is community response like this actually better?

I mean, both? The VA should absolutely have better systems for family notification. But also, this shows that when people know there's a need, they'll step up. The problem is usually that nobody knows.
Here's the thing though — the government's job isn't to manufacture community. It's to facilitate it. And in this case, they did. They made the space, they provided the honors, they let people know.
But Duke, what about the veterans who don't get this kind of turnout? What about the ones in rural areas where—
Look, you can't bureaucratize honor. You can't mandate that people care. This worked because real people decided it mattered to them.
I'm not saying bureaucratize it, I'm saying we should have better systems so fewer veterans end up isolated in the first place. This is beautiful, but it's also a symptom of a problem.
What's the broader issue here? Because this isn't just about veterans.

Social isolation. We have millions of Americans who are basically invisible until they die. Veterans, elderly people, people with disabilities, people who've been pushed out of their communities by economics.
But you can't solve loneliness with a federal program, Bree. This Tennessee thing worked because it was organic. People chose to show up.
Can I throw something weird into this? My neighbor is eighty-five and I literally only know this because his Amazon packages get delivered to my door. We've never had a real conversation.
That's exactly what I'm talking about. We live next to each other but we don't live with each other.
So go talk to him, Jax. Don't wait for the government to introduce you.
Oh, I will. But also, what if he's weird? What if he wants to show me his coin collection?
May I interject?
Please.
This Tennessee ceremony reminded me immediately of the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier. Same principle — community refusing to let service go unwitnessed.
That's a perfect transition, Professor. Take us to history class.

The Tomb of the Unknown Soldier was created after World War One, when tens of thousands of American servicemen couldn't be identified. Rather than let them disappear into statistics, we created a ritual.

November eleventh, nineteen twenty-one. They brought four caskets to Arlington. A decorated sergeant named Edward Younger chose one by placing white roses on it. That unknown soldier represents every unidentified service member.
So it's institutionalized community response?
Precisely. The Romans had a similar concept with their military honors. They believed a soldier who died unwitnessed died twice — once in battle, then again in forgetting.
That's heavy, Doc.

But here's what's remarkable about this Tennessee ceremony. It wasn't mandated by government. It wasn't a formal Unknown Soldier situation. Regular citizens decided, spontaneously, that this man would not be forgotten.
The Greeks called this 'kleos' — glory that lives beyond death through memory. These Tennessee folks gave Lonnie Wayman kleos. They made him immortal in the way that matters.
That's exactly right. Honor isn't a government program. It's something people give each other.

And yet, institutions matter too. Arlington exists because we decided, collectively, that some things require permanent witness. The question is when do we institutionalize and when do we trust community.
Okay but here's what gets me — this only happened because someone noticed and spread the word. How many Lonnie Waymans are there that nobody hears about?
That's the systemic question, isn't it? This was beautiful, but was it scalable?
History suggests that the most enduring honors combine both. Official recognition creates the framework. Community response provides the meaning. Neither works alone.
I'm definitely talking to my neighbor now. What if he has amazing stories and I've been missing out because I was afraid of small talk?
Alright, from honoring the dead to catching the living who are stealing from all of us. Bree, Duke — DOJ fraud division. Thoughts?
So Todd Blanche says they're going to prosecute anyone who steals over a thousand dollars from federal programs. As a deterrent.
Good. Four million dollars for fake homeless shelters? Two guys from Philly just driving to Minneapolis and robbing taxpayers blind? Prosecute them all.
I agree on the big cases, but prosecuting everyone over a thousand dollars? That seems like it could catch a lot of people who made mistakes rather than—
Bree, a thousand dollars isn't a mistake. That's intentional fraud. If you can't figure out whether you're stealing a thousand dollars, you shouldn't be handling federal money.
What's the scale of this problem? Because Blanche called it 'insane.'
According to the Government Accountability Office, federal programs lose about sixty billion dollars annually to improper payments. Not all fraud, but a significant portion is.
Sixty billion? I feel like an idiot for paying my taxes honestly.
But here's my concern — if you make the penalties too harsh, you might scare away legitimate applicants who are afraid of making paperwork errors.
Or maybe you scare away the fraudsters and the legitimate applicants get served better because there's more money to go around.
That assumes perfect enforcement, which we know doesn't exist. You'll end up with selective prosecution that hits—
Hits criminals? Good.

Hits people who don't have good lawyers, Duke. The Pennsylvania guys with four million? They probably have accountants and attorneys. The single mom who messes up her childcare voucher paperwork? She doesn't.
Then maybe we should fix the paperwork so it's not impossible to understand, instead of letting people steal because the system is broken.
That's actually a good point. Is the fraud problem a crime problem or an administration problem?
Both. You have organized criminals like the Philadelphia guys, but you also have programs that are so complicated that honest mistakes look like fraud.
And you have a culture where nobody expects to get caught because oversight is a joke. Blanche said they just send money like candy without checking anything.
Wait, can we talk about the fact that these guys drove from Philly to Minneapolis to commit fraud? Like, that's dedication. Evil dedication, but still.
It's also interstate commerce, which makes it clearly federal jurisdiction. No ambiguity about who prosecutes.
And it shows this isn't accidental. These guys researched programs, traveled hundreds of miles, forged documents. That's organized crime.
So where does that leave us on the thousand-dollar prosecution threshold?
I think intent matters more than amount. Go after the organized fraudsters hard. But maybe have different penalties for paperwork errors versus deliberate theft.
I can live with that, as long as the penalties are real. Right now, people steal because they know nothing will happen to them.
Can we also prosecute whatever genius designed these forms? Because I tried to apply for a small business loan once and I'm pretty sure it was written in ancient Sumerian.
From fraud to the future — AI poll results. Jax, this feels like your territory.

So eighty-seven percent of Americans think AI will transform our lives, but only forty-seven percent find it useful right now. That's like saying 'this hurricane will destroy everything, but today it's just windy.'
I think people are rightfully skeptical. Most AI they encounter is either trying to sell them something or making weird mistakes.
I asked ChatGPT to help me fix my lawn mower last week. It told me to check if it was plugged in. It's a gas mower.
Did you try plugging it in though? Maybe AI is just thinking outside the box.
The interesting finding is that eighty-nine percent want AI content clearly labeled. That's almost universal agreement, which is rare.
Because people want to know when they're being manipulated. AI-generated content feels deceptive if you don't know it's AI.
It's basic honesty. If a robot wrote it, tell me a robot wrote it. Don't pretend it's human.
But what if the robot is a better writer than the human? Like, what if AI writes a more compelling news article than—
Jax.
I'm just saying, maybe we should label human content too. 'Warning: This article was written by someone who drinks too much coffee and has opinions.'
The trust issue is real though. Eighty percent are concerned AI is eroding trust in news and social media.
Which is funny because I already don't trust news and social media. AI can't make it worse.
Thanks for that vote of confidence in our industry, Duke.
Present company excluded, obviously.
This reminds me of the introduction of television in the nineteen fifties.
How so, Professor?

People knew television would change everything, but they couldn't predict how. They worried about the wrong things — would it kill radio? Would children go blind? — while missing the real impacts on politics and social cohesion.
So we're worrying about AI taking our jobs while missing how it might change how we think about truth?
Precisely. The technology transforms the medium, and the medium transforms us. McLuhan was right — the medium is the message.
Great, so AI will change how we think, and also seventy-four percent of Americans think aliens exist. We're going to be very confused when the space robots arrive.

Speaking of confused, time for Rapid Fire, where today's news gets weird. First up — Palantir, the AI company that helps the government spy on people, is now using its artificial intelligence to... find office chairs for government employees.
From global surveillance to office furniture. That's quite a pivot.
I mean, have you seen government procurement? They probably need AI to figure out why a chair costs three thousand dollars.
Next — an Irish employee won a workplace harassment case because his boss repeatedly shouted 'potato' at him. The boss claimed it was motivational. The court disagreed.
How is 'potato' motivational?
I have no idea, but I'm definitely using this at my next performance review. 'Leo, you need to potato harder.'
That's not how motivation works.

Louisiana classrooms have until Friday to post the Ten Commandments, as required by new state law. Schools are scrambling to find poster boards and wondering if Comic Sans is appropriate for divine law.
I have so many constitutional questions about this.
The Supreme Court will likely have opinions as well.
And finally, a Florida drug dealer locked a man in a storage unit for months over an unpaid debt. The victim was eventually found by someone who heard yelling from inside the unit.
That's kidnapping. That's very serious kidnapping.
Yeah, that one's not funny. Florida, get your act together.
Florida's been Florida long before we started noticing.
True, but now they have AI to help them be Florida more efficiently.

Alright, time for The Daily Do. After that Tennessee story, I looked up whether there are similar ceremonies in other areas. Turns out, many VA cemeteries hold quarterly services for unclaimed veterans.

So today's Daily Do: check if there's an unclaimed veteran ceremony in your area this month. They're usually listed on your regional VA website under 'memorial services.' Most of these ceremonies need attendance — that's the whole point.
That's really beautiful, Leo. It's like the Tennessee thing, but ongoing.
And it doesn't require any special skills or knowledge. You just show up and stand there respectfully.
I'm actually going to do this one. After hearing about Lonnie Wayman, it feels important.
As Pericles said in his funeral oration, 'The whole earth is the tomb of heroic men.' But it helps when the living remember to visit.
Professor, that's perfect. Any other parting wisdom?
From Marcus Aurelius: 'Death smiles at us all, but all a man can do is smile back.' Tennessee smiled back for Lonnie Wayman.
That's The Friction for Monday, March tenth. I'm Jax, along with Leo, Professor Pringle, Bree, and Duke. We'll be back tomorrow to figure out what else is broken and who's trying to fix it.
And hey — talk to your neighbors. Even if they want to show you their coin collection. Especially if they want to show you their coin collection.
Thanks for listening. Take care of each other.
